GS2.headline: Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) Adoption Soars, Government Plans Global Outreach for India Stack
GS2.explainer:
• India's Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI), particularly the India Stack, has seen unprecedented domestic adoption and efficiency gains.
• The government announced plans for a global outreach program to share India's DPI expertise and technology with other developing nations.
• The initiative aims to support countries in building their own digital ecosystems, fostering financial inclusion and efficient public service delivery.
• Key components like Aadhaar, UPI, and DigiLocker are being showcased as successful models for digital transformation.
• This move positions India as a leader in digital governance and a proponent of open-source, inclusive digital solutions.
gsSubTopics: E-governance- applications, models, successes, limitations, and potential; citizens charters, transparency & accountability and institutional and other measures.
Government Policies and Interventions for Development
prelimsPoints: India Stack is a set of APIs that allows governments, businesses, startups and developers to utilize a unique digital infrastructure.
Key components include identity (Aadhaar), payments (UPI), and data exchange (DigiLocker).
DPI aims to provide public goods for digital economy and governance.
Many countries have expressed interest in adopting India's DPI model.
It promotes financial inclusion by enabling digital transactions for all.
mainsPoints: Discuss the transformative impact of India's Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) on governance, financial inclusion, and service delivery.
Analyze the potential benefits and challenges of India's global outreach program for sharing its DPI expertise with other developing nations.
Evaluate how the India Stack model can foster inclusive digital transformation in countries with diverse socio-economic contexts.
Examine the ethical considerations and data privacy concerns associated with large-scale DPI implementation.
Critically assess India's role as a leader in digital governance and its contribution to global digital cooperation.
GS2.imageDescription: A network of interconnected digital icons representing various components of the India Stack (Aadhaar, UPI, DigiLocker) with a globe in the background.
GS2.trivia: UPI processed over 10 billion transactions in a single month in 2023, showcasing its massive scale.

GS2.headline: Parliamentary Panel Recommends Reforms in Election Funding Laws to Enhance Transparency
GS2.explainer:
• A bipartisan Parliamentary Standing Committee submitted its report recommending significant reforms to India's election funding laws.
• The report advocated for greater transparency in political donations, including stricter disclosure norms for anonymous contributions.
• Suggestions included reviewing the electoral bond scheme and exploring alternatives to ensure accountability and level playing field.
• The panel emphasized the need for a robust regulatory framework to curb the influence of money power in elections.
• The recommendations are expected to spark a fresh debate on electoral reforms and democratic accountability.
gsSubTopics: Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act.
Appointment to various Constitutional posts, powers, functions and responsibilities of various Constitutional Bodies.
Electoral Reforms.
prelimsPoints: Election funding in India is regulated by the Representation of the People Act, 1951.
Electoral Bonds were introduced in 2018 to allow anonymous donations to political parties.
The Election Commission of India (ECI) has often advocated for greater transparency in funding.
Parliamentary Standing Committees examine legislative proposals and government policies.
The issue of money power in elections is a persistent challenge for Indian democracy.
mainsPoints: Discuss the challenges posed by opaque election funding to the integrity of democratic processes in India.
Analyze the recommendations of the Parliamentary Panel for reforming election funding laws and their potential impact on transparency.
Evaluate the pros and cons of the electoral bond scheme and explore alternative mechanisms for political donations that ensure accountability.
Examine the role of the Election Commission of India and civil society organizations in advocating for electoral reforms.
Critically assess the balance between protecting donor privacy and ensuring transparency in political funding.
GS2.imageDescription: A ballot box with currency notes, symbolizing the issue of money in elections, placed in front of a Parliament building.
GS2.trivia: The Association for Democratic Reforms (ADR) is a prominent NGO working on electoral and political reforms in India.

GS2.headline: UN Security Council Reform Debate Gains Momentum Ahead of 2025 Summit
GS2.explainer:
• The debate on reforming the United Nations Security Council (UNSC) has gained renewed momentum, with several nations pushing for expansion of permanent and non-permanent seats.
• India, a strong contender for a permanent seat, reiterated its call for a more representative and equitable UNSC structure.
• Discussions focused on the need to reflect contemporary geopolitical realities and enhance the council's legitimacy and effectiveness.
• Key proposals include increasing membership from Asia, Africa, and Latin America, and reforming the veto power.
• The upcoming 2025 UN Summit is seen as a critical window for potential breakthroughs in the long-stalled reform process.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate.
prelimsPoints: The UNSC has 5 permanent members (P5: China, France, Russia, UK, US) with veto power.
It also has 10 non-permanent members elected for two-year terms.
India has been a non-permanent member multiple times and seeks a permanent seat.
The G4 nations (India, Brazil, Germany, Japan) advocate for permanent membership for each other.
UNSC reform aims for greater representation, effectiveness, and legitimacy.
mainsPoints: Discuss the historical context and contemporary relevance of the demand for reforms in the United Nations Security Council (UNSC).
Analyze India's arguments for securing a permanent seat in the UNSC and the challenges it faces in achieving this objective.
Evaluate the implications of the veto power for the effectiveness and democratic functioning of the UNSC.
Examine the various proposals for UNSC reform and the positions of key member states on these proposals.
Critically assess the potential impact of a reformed UNSC on global governance, peace, and security.
GS2.imageDescription: The United Nations logo with a stylized image of the Security Council chamber.
GS2.trivia: The last major reform of the UNSC took place in 1965, increasing non-permanent seats from 6 to 10.

GS2.headline: Parliamentary Winter Session Concludes with Passage of Key Bills and Debates on Economic Issues
GS2.explainer:
• The Winter Session of Parliament concluded, witnessing the passage of several important legislations across various sectors.
• Key bills included amendments to social security codes, a new framework for digital data protection, and reforms in agricultural marketing.
• Debates centered on pressing economic issues such as inflation, unemployment, and government fiscal policy, with opposition raising concerns.
• The session also saw discussions on international relations, climate change commitments, and internal security challenges.
• Despite procedural disruptions, the session contributed to the legislative agenda and parliamentary oversight.
gsSubTopics: Parliament and State Legislatures—structure, functioning, conduct of business, powers & privileges and issues arising out of these.
prelimsPoints: Parliament has three main sessions: Budget, Monsoon, and Winter sessions.
Bills become acts after being passed by both houses and receiving presidential assent.
Parliamentary committees play a crucial role in scrutinizing bills.
Opposition often uses parliamentary tools like calling attention motions and adjournment motions.
Key functions of Parliament include law-making, financial control, and deliberative functions.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of parliamentary sessions in a democratic setup for law-making, accountability, and deliberation.
Analyze the major legislative achievements and key debates during the recently concluded Winter Session of Parliament.
Evaluate the effectiveness of parliamentary oversight mechanisms in holding the executive accountable.
Examine the challenges faced by the Indian Parliament, such as disruptions, declining debate quality, and the role of anti-defection law.
Suggest reforms to enhance the productivity and efficiency of parliamentary proceedings in India.
GS2.imageDescription: The Parliament House building in New Delhi, illuminated at dusk.
GS2.trivia: The longest parliamentary session in India was the first Lok Sabha's Budget Session in 1952, which lasted for 107 days.

GS2.headline: India Initiates Review of Indus Water Treaty Provisions Amidst Growing Water Scarcity Concerns
GS2.explainer:
• India has initiated a comprehensive review of certain provisions of the Indus Water Treaty (IWT) with Pakistan, citing concerns over water scarcity and climate change impacts.
• The review aims to explore optimal utilization of India's share of water resources from the eastern rivers, as permitted under the treaty.
• Discussions are expected to focus on technical aspects related to run-of-the-river hydropower projects and agricultural usage.
• This move comes amidst increasing demands for water in India's upstream regions and calls for a more equitable water sharing mechanism.
• While affirming commitment to the treaty, India seeks to ensure its rights are fully exercised in the face of changing hydrological realities.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: The Indus Water Treaty (IWT) was signed in 1960 between India and Pakistan, brokered by the World Bank.
It allocates the waters of the Indus River system, comprising six rivers.
Eastern Rivers (Ravi, Beas, Sutlej) are for India's unrestricted use.
Western Rivers (Indus, Jhelum, Chenab) are largely for Pakistan's unrestricted use, with limited non-consumptive uses for India.
The Permanent Indus Commission (PIC) is a bilateral mechanism for implementation and dispute resolution.
mainsPoints: Discuss the historical background and key provisions of the Indus Water Treaty (IWT) and its significance for India-Pakistan relations.
Analyze the reasons behind India's decision to review certain provisions of the IWT, considering factors like water scarcity and climate change.
Evaluate the potential implications of this review for bilateral relations between India and Pakistan and regional water management.
Examine India's rights and responsibilities under the IWT regarding the utilization of eastern and western river waters.
Critically assess the role of international treaties in managing transboundary water resources and the challenges of adapting them to new environmental realities.
GS2.imageDescription: A map showing the Indus River system flowing through India and Pakistan, highlighting the eastern and western rivers.
GS2.trivia: The Indus Water Treaty is often cited as one of the most successful water-sharing treaties in the world, having survived multiple conflicts between India and Pakistan.

GS3.headline: RBI Maintains Repo Rate, Signals Cautious Stance Amid Global Headwinds and Domestic Inflation Concerns
GS3.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) unanimously decided to keep the repo rate unchanged at 6.5%.
• The decision reflects a cautious approach to balance inflation control with supporting economic growth, amidst global uncertainties.
• RBI highlighted ongoing concerns about volatile food inflation and geopolitical risks impacting commodity prices.
• The central bank reiterated its commitment to withdrawing accommodation to ensure inflation aligns with the target while supporting growth.
• Analysts anticipate the RBI to maintain its stance for the near term, with potential rate cuts dependent on sustained disinflation.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Monetary Policy.
prelimsPoints: Repo rate is the rate at which RBI lends money to commercial banks.
The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) determines the policy rates (repo, reverse repo, etc.).
RBI's primary objective is to maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth.
Inflation targeting framework in India aims for a 4% CPI with a +/- 2% band.
Global headwinds include geopolitical tensions, supply chain disruptions, and crude oil volatility.
mainsPoints: Analyze the rationale behind the RBI's decision to maintain the repo rate amidst current economic conditions.
Discuss the impact of monetary policy decisions on various sectors of the Indian economy, including investment, consumption, and credit growth.
Evaluate the challenges faced by the RBI in balancing inflation control and supporting economic growth in a volatile global environment.
Examine the effectiveness of India's inflation targeting framework and suggest potential improvements.
Critically assess the role of global factors (geopolitics, commodity prices) in shaping India's domestic monetary policy.
GS3.imageDescription: The Reserve Bank of India logo with a graph showing stable interest rates.
GS3.trivia: The Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) in India consists of six members: three officials from the RBI and three external members nominated by the government.

GS3.headline: National Green Hydrogen Mission Progress: Government Announces New Projects and Incentives for Production
GS3.explainer:
• The Union Government announced significant progress in the National Green Hydrogen Mission, unveiling new projects and an enhanced incentive framework.
• The new projects focus on scaling up green hydrogen production, developing infrastructure for storage and transportation, and promoting demand in key sectors.
• The enhanced incentive scheme aims to attract private investment and reduce the cost of green hydrogen production, making it competitive.
• Focus areas include green hydrogen applications in steel, fertilizer, refining, and mobility sectors to drive decarbonization efforts.
• India aims to become a global hub for green hydrogen production and export, contributing to energy security and climate goals.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Infrastructure: Energy.
prelimsPoints: Green hydrogen is produced by electrolysis of water using renewable energy.
The National Green Hydrogen Mission aims for 5 million tonnes (MT) of green hydrogen production by 2030.
Key components include SIGHT (Strategic Interventions for Green Hydrogen Transition) Programme.
Applications are in hard-to-abate sectors like steel, cement, and ammonia.
India aims to reduce fossil fuel imports and achieve net-zero emissions by 2070.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of the National Green Hydrogen Mission for India's energy security and climate goals.
Analyze the economic and environmental benefits of transitioning to a green hydrogen economy in India.
Evaluate the challenges in scaling up green hydrogen production and adoption, including technological, financial, and infrastructural hurdles.
Examine the role of government incentives and private sector investment in achieving the mission's targets.
Critically assess India's potential to become a global leader in green hydrogen production and export.
GS3.imageDescription: An illustration showing hydrogen molecules being produced from water using wind turbines and solar panels, symbolizing green hydrogen production.
GS3.trivia: India's target for green hydrogen production by 2030 is 5 million metric tonnes (MMT).

GS3.headline: ISRO Successfully Tests Next-Generation Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) Technology Demonstrator
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) achieved a significant milestone with the successful test flight of its Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) Technology Demonstrator.
• The test involved a precise landing experiment, mimicking the return of a spacecraft from orbit, demonstrating autonomous landing capabilities.
• This critical step brings India closer to developing a fully reusable two-stage orbital launch vehicle, aiming to reduce space access costs.
• The RLV program is crucial for future space exploration, satellite deployment, and human spaceflight missions like Gaganyaan.
• The technology is expected to revolutionize India's space capabilities, making launches more frequent and economical.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of Space.
prelimsPoints: RLV-TD is a series of technology demonstration missions for ISRO's RLV program.
The goal is to achieve cost-effective and reliable access to space.
Key technologies demonstrated include hypersonic flight, autonomous landing, and winged body aerodynamics.
RLV technology is crucial for future human spaceflight (Gaganyaan) and advanced satellite launches.
Other countries developing similar technology include USA (SpaceX, NASA) and Europe (ESA).
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic importance of Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) technology for India's space program and its global competitiveness.
Analyze the economic and technological benefits of reducing space access costs through RLV development.
Evaluate the challenges involved in developing and operationalizing RLV technology, including material science, propulsion, and guidance systems.
Examine how RLV technology can contribute to India's future space exploration missions and commercial space industry.
Critically assess India's position in the global race for reusable space technology and compare it with other spacefaring nations.
GS3.imageDescription: An artist's impression of ISRO's Reusable Launch Vehicle (RLV) gliding back to Earth for a landing.
GS3.trivia: ISRO first tested the RLV-TD in 2016, performing a hypersonic flight experiment (HEX).

GS3.headline: India Strengthens Cyber Security Framework, Launches National Cyber Resilience Strategy 2024
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) unveiled India's comprehensive National Cyber Resilience Strategy 2024.
• The strategy aims to create a robust, resilient, and secure cyberspace for all citizens and critical infrastructure.
• Key pillars include enhancing cyber deterrence, promoting public-private partnerships, and fostering a culture of cyber hygiene.
• Focus areas involve protecting critical information infrastructure, combating cybercrime, and developing indigenous cyber security capabilities.
• The initiative underscores India's commitment to tackling evolving cyber threats and ensuring digital sovereignty.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate.
Cyber Security.
prelimsPoints: CERT-In (Indian Computer Emergency Response Team) is the national agency for cyber security incident response.
The strategy covers protection of critical infrastructure like banking, power, and telecom.
Focus on indigenous development of cyber security products and services.
Public-private partnerships are crucial for effective cyber defense.
Cyber hygiene refers to practices for maintaining system health and security.
mainsPoints: Discuss the growing threat of cyber-attacks and their implications for national security and economic stability in India.
Analyze the key provisions and objectives of the National Cyber Resilience Strategy 2024.
Evaluate the challenges in implementing a comprehensive cyber security framework, including technological, human resource, and legal aspects.
Examine the role of public-private partnerships and international cooperation in enhancing India's cyber resilience.
Critically assess the balance between national security concerns and individual privacy rights in the context of cyber security measures.
GS3.imageDescription: A stylized digital shield icon, representing cyber security, overlaid on a map of India.
GS3.trivia: India is one of the top countries experiencing cyberattacks globally, making robust cyber security critical.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Internal Security
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Challenges to Internal Security through Communication Networks

GS3.headline: India Pledges Strong Support for Global Methane Emission Reduction Initiative
GS3.explainer:
• India reaffirmed its commitment to combating climate change by pledging stronger support for international initiatives aimed at reducing methane emissions.
• The announcement came during a virtual summit on climate action, emphasizing methane's potent greenhouse gas effect.
• India highlighted its ongoing domestic efforts in sectors like agriculture, waste management, and energy to control methane leakage and emissions.
• The pledge involves enhancing measurement, reporting, and verification (MRV) systems for methane and exploring new mitigation technologies.
• This move aligns with India's updated Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) and its goal of achieving net-zero emissions by 2070.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Climate Change.
GS3.source: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change
prelimsPoints: Methane (CH4) is a potent greenhouse gas, much more effective at trapping heat than CO2 over a short period.
Major sources include agriculture (livestock, rice cultivation), waste (landfills), and fossil fuel production.
India has not formally joined the Global Methane Pledge but has strong domestic actions.
Reducing methane can have a quicker impact on warming than CO2 due to its shorter atmospheric lifespan.
MRV (Measurement, Reporting, and Verification) systems are critical for tracking emissions.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of methane emission reduction in achieving global climate goals and limiting global warming to 1.5°C.
Analyze India's domestic strategies and policies for mitigating methane emissions across various sectors.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities for India in enhancing its contribution to global methane reduction initiatives.
Examine the socio-economic implications of methane mitigation efforts, particularly in the agricultural sector.
Critically assess the role of international cooperation and technology transfer in supporting developing countries' methane reduction targets.
GS3.imageDescription: A graphic showing sources of methane emissions (livestock, landfills, oil & gas) and their impact on global warming.
GS3.trivia: Methane has a global warming potential (GWP) 28-34 times greater than CO2 over a 100-year period.

GS3.headline: Government Extends Minimum Support Price (MSP) for Rabi Crops, Focus on Farmer Welfare and Food Security
GS3.explainer:
• The Union Cabinet approved the extension of Minimum Support Price (MSP) for all mandated Rabi crops for the upcoming marketing season.
• The decision aims to ensure remunerative prices for farmers, incentivize crop diversification, and strengthen agricultural income.
• The MSP hike reflects the government's commitment to implementing the 'A2+FL' formula, ensuring at least 1.5 times the cost of production.
• This move is expected to bolster rural demand, stabilize food grain prices, and contribute to national food security objectives.
• The government also emphasized improvements in procurement mechanisms to ensure effective reach and benefit to small and marginal farmers.
gsSubTopics: Major crops - cropping patterns in various parts of the country, - different types of irrigation and irrigation systems storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.

Issues related to direct and indirect farm subsidies and minimum support prices; Public Distribution System— objectives, functioning, limitations, revamping; issues of buffer stocks and food security.
prelimsPoints: Rabi crops are winter crops sown in October-November and harvested in March-April (e.g., wheat, barley, gram, mustard).
MSP is a guaranteed price for farmers' produce, announced by the government.
The Commission for Agricultural Costs and Prices (CACP) recommends MSPs.
A2+FL formula includes actual paid out cost (A2) plus imputed value of family labour (FL).
MSP aims to protect farmers from market fluctuations and ensure food security.
mainsPoints: Discuss the objectives and significance of the Minimum Support Price (MSP) regime in ensuring farmer welfare and food security in India.
Analyze the economic implications of MSP on agricultural production, market prices, and government finances.
Evaluate the effectiveness of the 'A2+FL' formula in providing remunerative prices to farmers and addressing their distress.
Examine the challenges associated with MSP implementation, including procurement disparities, market distortions, and environmental sustainability.
Suggest reforms to the MSP system that can make it more equitable, efficient, and aligned with sustainable agricultural practices.
GS3.imageDescription: A farmer in a wheat field, symbolizing agricultural production and farmer welfare.
GS3.trivia: Currently, the government announces MSP for 22 mandated crops and Fair and Remunerative Price (FRP) for sugarcane.

GS3.headline: New Indigenous Vaccine for Dengue Shows Promising Results in Advanced Clinical Trials
GS3.explainer:
• Indian researchers announced promising results from advanced clinical trials of a new indigenously developed vaccine for dengue fever.
• The multi-center Phase III trials demonstrated high efficacy and a strong safety profile across diverse age groups and geographical regions.
• The vaccine targets all four serotypes of the dengue virus, offering broad protection against the vector-borne disease.
• This breakthrough is crucial for India, which bears a significant burden of dengue cases annually, especially during monsoon seasons.
• Regulatory approval is anticipated in the coming months, paving the way for large-scale production and public health deployment.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health.
prelimsPoints: Dengue is a mosquito-borne viral infection caused by the dengue virus (DENV).
There are four distinct serotypes of the virus (DENV-1, DENV-2, DENV-3, DENV-4).
The disease is transmitted by Aedes aegypti mosquitoes.
Symptoms range from mild fever to severe dengue (dengue hemorrhagic fever, dengue shock syndrome).
ICMR (Indian Council of Medical Research) is a leading body for health research in India.
mainsPoints: Discuss the public health significance of developing an indigenous vaccine for dengue fever in India.
Analyze the challenges involved in vaccine development and deployment for vector-borne diseases like dengue.
Evaluate the potential impact of this vaccine on reducing the disease burden and improving health outcomes in India.
Examine the role of scientific research and indigenous innovation in addressing India's health challenges.
Critically assess the strategies for effective vaccine distribution and achieving widespread immunization against dengue.
GS3.imageDescription: A microscopic image of the dengue virus, with a syringe and vaccine vial in the foreground.
GS3.trivia: Dengue is also known as 'breakbone fever' due to the severe muscle and joint pains it causes.

GS3.headline: National Logistics Policy Implementation Review: Focus on Multimodal Connectivity and Cost Reduction
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Commerce and Industry conducted a comprehensive review of the National Logistics Policy (NLP) implementation, highlighting key achievements and future targets.
• The review emphasized significant progress in enhancing multimodal connectivity through PM Gati Shakti National Master Plan.
• Efforts are underway to further reduce logistics costs as a percentage of GDP, aiming to boost India's global competitiveness.
• Focus areas include digitalization of logistics processes, development of skilled manpower, and promotion of green logistics practices.
• The policy aims to create a seamlessly integrated, cost-efficient, and resilient logistics ecosystem in India.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
prelimsPoints: National Logistics Policy was launched in 2022 to reduce logistics costs and improve efficiency.
PM Gati Shakti is a national master plan for multimodal connectivity infrastructure projects.
Logistics cost in India is currently estimated at 13-14% of GDP, higher than global average.
Aims to create a single window e-logistics marketplace.
Focus on integrating various modes of transport (road, rail, air, waterways).
mainsPoints: Discuss the objectives and key pillars of India's National Logistics Policy (NLP) and its significance for economic competitiveness.
Analyze how the PM Gati Shakti National Master Plan contributes to improving multimodal connectivity and reducing logistics costs.
Evaluate the challenges in achieving a seamlessly integrated and efficient logistics ecosystem in India.
Examine the role of digitalization, skill development, and green logistics in transforming India's supply chain.
Critically assess the impact of reduced logistics costs on various sectors of the economy, including manufacturing, agriculture, and exports.
GS3.imageDescription: A visual representation of multimodal transport, showing trucks, trains, ships, and airplanes connected by digital lines.
GS3.trivia: India aims to bring down logistics costs to global benchmarks of 8-10% of GDP.

GS3.headline: Major Nations Agree on Phased Reduction of Fossil Fuels at International Climate Forum
GS3.explainer:
• Leading industrial and developing nations reached a consensus on a phased reduction of fossil fuels at a high-level international climate forum.
• The agreement outlines a roadmap for transitioning away from coal, oil, and gas, with differentiated responsibilities for developed and developing countries.
• It emphasizes investments in renewable energy, energy efficiency, and low-carbon technologies to achieve the phase-down targets.
• The pact also includes provisions for financial and technological support to developing nations for their energy transition.
• This landmark accord is seen as a crucial step towards achieving the Paris Agreement goals and limiting global warming to 1.5°C.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Climate Change.
India and its neighborhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: Fossil fuels (coal, oil, gas) are the largest source of greenhouse gas emissions.
The Paris Agreement aims to limit global warming to well below 2°C, preferably to 1.5°C.
CBDR principle acknowledges different capabilities and historical responsibilities of countries.
Energy transition involves shifting from fossil fuels to cleaner energy sources.
Financial and technological support for developing nations is a key demand.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of the agreement on phased reduction of fossil fuels for global climate action and its implications for India's energy policy.
Analyze the concept of 'Common But Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities' (CBDR-RC) in the context of international climate agreements.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities for developing countries, including India, in transitioning away from fossil fuels while ensuring energy security and economic growth.
Examine the role of international finance and technology transfer in facilitating a just energy transition globally.
Critically assess the feasibility and timeline for achieving a complete phase-out of fossil fuels, considering geopolitical and economic realities.
GS3.imageDescription: A graphic showing a globe with renewable energy sources (solar panels, wind turbines) replacing fossil fuel power plants.
GS3.trivia: The first explicit mention of 'transitioning away' from fossil fuels appeared in the COP28 agreement in Dubai.

GS3.headline: Namami Gange Mission Achieves Significant Milestones in River Rejuvenation and Public Participation
GS3.explainer:
• The Namami Gange Mission reported achieving significant milestones in its efforts to clean and rejuvenate the Ganga River and its tributaries.
• Key achievements include the operationalization of numerous sewage treatment plants (STPs), reducing industrial pollution, and improving river biodiversity.
• The mission emphasized increased public participation through 'Ganga Praharis' and awareness campaigns for sustainable river management.
• Focus areas for the next phase involve expanding coverage to smaller towns, promoting water-efficient agriculture, and protecting riverine ecosystems.
• The success of Namami Gange is seen as a model for holistic river basin management and environmental conservation in India.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Government Policies and Interventions for Development in various sectors.
prelimsPoints: Namami Gange is an integrated conservation mission, approved by the Union Government in 2014.
It aims for effective abatement of pollution and conservation and rejuvenation of the National River Ganga.
Key pillars include sewage infrastructure, industrial effluent monitoring, river front development, and biodiversity conservation.
National Mission for Clean Ganga (NMCG) implements the mission.
Ganga Praharis are self-motivated volunteers working for Ganga conservation.
mainsPoints: Discuss the multi-faceted approach of the Namami Gange Mission for river rejuvenation and its significance for environmental conservation in India.
Analyze the challenges encountered in cleaning the Ganga River, including industrial pollution, municipal waste, and agricultural runoff.
Evaluate the role of infrastructure development (STPs), policy interventions, and public participation in achieving the mission's objectives.
Examine how the success of Namami Gange can serve as a blueprint for managing other polluted river basins in the country.
Critically assess the sustainability of the mission's achievements and outline future strategies for long-term river health.
GS3.imageDescription: A clean stretch of the Ganga River with a ghat and people, symbolizing successful rejuvenation efforts.
GS3.trivia: The Ganga River basin is the largest in India, covering over 25% of the country's land area.

sources: Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change
